findings.title: StreamEvent 'finish' variant uses `& any`, destroying type safety
findings.description: Encapsulation: N/A, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 2/10 (Aggregate: 3.3). The StreamEvent discriminated union has `{ type: 'finish' } & any` for its finish variant. Intersecting with `any` erases all type information -- the compiler will accept any property access on finish events without error. This defeats the purpose of a discriminated union, which is to make illegal states unrepresentable. The finish event should have explicit fields (e.g., `finishReason: string`) rather than falling back to `any`. This is pre-existing code, but the new `streamFromAiSdk` function relies on this type for its `onFinish` callback, so the unsafety propagates into the new code.
findings.suggestion: Replace `({ type: 'finish' } & any)` with a properly typed variant: `{ type: 'finish', finishReason: string }` (or whatever fields the finish event actually carries). This will restore compile-time checking for finish event consumers in both the xsAI and AI SDK streaming paths.

findings.title: AiSdkModelConfig.provider is bare `string` instead of literal union
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 4.25). The `provider` field in AiSdkModelConfig is typed as `string | undefined`, but the implementation in `createAiSdkModel` only branches on `'anthropic'` vs everything-else (treated as OpenAI-compatible). This is classic primitive obsession: a domain concept (SDK provider) is represented as an unconstrained string when only a small set of values are meaningful. Passing `provider: 'gooogle'` (typo) compiles silently and falls through to the OpenAI path, which would produce confusing runtime errors.
findings.suggestion: Type the field as a literal union: `provider?: 'anthropic' | 'openai'`. This makes the valid values self-documenting and gives compile-time feedback on typos. If more providers are added later, they can be added to the union.

findings.title: Multiple `as unknown as` casts bypass type system at SDK boundary
findings.code_snippet:
const chatConfig = chatProvider.chat(model) as unknown as Record<string, unknown>
...
messages: sanitized as unknown as import('ai').ModelMessage[]
...
result: chunk.output as string
findings.description: Encapsulation: 4/10, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 4/10, Enforcement: 2/10 (Aggregate: 3.25). The `streamFromAiSdk` function contains three unsafe double-casts (`as unknown as X`) to bridge between xsAI and AI SDK types. While SDK boundary adaptation inherently involves type bridging, double-casting through `unknown` silences all compiler warnings and defeats structural type checking. If either SDK changes its types, these casts will silently produce runtime errors. The `chatProvider.chat(model) as unknown as Record<string, unknown>` cast is particularly risky because it accesses `baseURL` and `apiKey` as dynamic string keys with no compile-time verification.
findings.suggestion: Create a typed adapter function that extracts the needed fields from the ChatProvider result and validates them at runtime: `function extractChatConfig(provider: ReturnType<ChatProvider['chat']>): AiSdkModelConfig`. Use Valibot to validate the extracted config shape. For the messages cast, create a `convertMessages(messages: Message[]): ModelMessage[]` function that does explicit field mapping rather than casting.

findings.title: StreamOptions.useAiSdk boolean flag controls code path dispatch
findings.description: Encapsulation: 6/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 6/10 (Aggregate: 5.75). Adding `useAiSdk?: boolean` to StreamOptions creates a boolean flag that dispatches between two entirely different streaming implementations (xsAI vs AI SDK). Boolean-flag dispatch is a mild type design smell: the caller must know which implementation to request, and the two paths have subtly different behaviors (e.g., different finishReason values: 'tool_calls' vs 'tool-calls'). A strategy pattern or separate function entry points would be more explicit. However, for a feature-flag-style migration between SDKs this is pragmatically reasonable.
findings.suggestion: Consider creating separate entry points: `streamViaXsai()` and `streamViaAiSdk()` instead of routing via boolean flag. Alternatively, if the flag is temporary during migration, document its intended removal timeline.

positive_observations: Good refactoring: tool resolution logic extracted into shared `resolveXsaiTools` function, reused by both xsAI and AI SDK paths -- avoids duplication
AiSdkModelConfig interface cleanly separates the configuration concerns from the model creation logic in createAiSdkModel
The convertXsaiToolsToAiSdk function is a pure adapter with clear input/output types (XsaiTool[] -> ToolSet), following the Functional Core pattern
TODO comments removed and replaced with descriptive comments (sanitizeMessages, tool compatibility errors)
